exemplar.title: To what extent does the mass added to an inflated balloon affect the terminal velocity of the falling balloon?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the mass added to an inflated balloon affect the terminal velocity of the falling balloon?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing steps are communicated clearly, with comprehensive raw and processed tables including uncertainties.
Calculations of averages, terminal velocities, and percentage differences are carried out correctly and annotated with clear mathematics.
Hypotheses are clearly stated, and appropriate graph(s) reinforce the statistical framework.
weaknesses: Propagation of uncertainties omits several derived quantities (e.g., radius, drop height), leaving some gaps in uncertainty analysis.
Graphical communication lacks precision: missing axis error bars and incomplete labels reduce scientific clarity.
Minor typographical errors in key formulae (e.g., terminal velocity expression) compromise precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates generally accurate and appropriate data processing with clear communication of methods, earning an upper-band performance. The consideration of uncertainties shows appropriate intent but with significant omissions, placing the work at the high end of Band 3â4. Taken together, the clarity and largely accurate processing justify a score of 5 out of 6 in Data Analysis.

strengths: Research question is situated in a specific and appropriate scientific and personal context, demonstrating deep engagement.
Apparatus and procedure are described in sufficient detail to allow replication, with only minor ambiguities.
Null and alternative hypotheses are clearly stated and mathematically expressed, providing a robust framework.
weaknesses: Controlled-variables table repeats rationale without specifying independent management of each variable.
Inconsistencies in drop heights (1 m vs. 2.5 m) introduce minor reproducibility ambiguity.
Methodological considerations are described but could be more fully explained in terms of how they influence data sufficiency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs research design excels in contextualising the research question within parachute physics and personal experience, showing mastery in Band 5â6 for context. The methodology is largely complete and reproducible, with only a few minor ambiguities, justifying a topâband determination. While methodological considerations are well described, they fall just short of the full explanatory depth; however, the overall strength of the design supports a full-mark allocation in light of the high-quality contextualisation and replicable procedure.

strengths: The conclusion directly addresses the research question and is quantitatively linked to both experimental data and theoretical expectations.
Interpretation of results is justified and consistent with observed trends in terminal velocity versus mass.
Integration of Stokesâ law and Newtonâs second law strengthens the scientific argument.
weaknesses: Comparison to the accepted scientific context is somewhat superficial, lacking a full discussion of the causes behind the ~55% discrepancy.
Statistical rigor (e.g., p-value or regression metrics) is mentioned but not fully detailed in the conclusion.
Discussion of drag coefficient assumptions remains brief and could be expanded.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs conclusion is well supported by analysis, demonstrating justification and consistency with the data (Band 5â6). However, the comparison to accepted scientific values lacks depth, and statistical details are underdeveloped, preventing a perfect score. A score of 5 appropriately reflects a robust but not fully exhaustive conclusion.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (reaction-time error, balloon asymmetry, turbulence) are identified and described.
Suggested improvements (video analysis, refined measurement techniques) are realistic and relevant to the investigationâs context.
The evaluation demonstrates awareness of how experimental design could be refined.
weaknesses: Relative impact of identified weaknesses on the results is not explained or quantified.
Improvement suggestions are described but lack detailed explanation of how they reduce errors quantitatively.
Safety and environmental considerations are noted generally but omit specific controls for indoor air currents and lighting.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student describes both methodological weaknesses and realistic improvements, placing the work solidly in the Band 3â4 range for Evaluation. While identification and description are clear, the evaluation stops short of explaining the relative impact of weaknesses or quantitatively justifying improvements. A score of 4 reflects a competent but not fully developed evaluative section.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated, linked to parachute physics and sky-diving safety, and the student situates it within the forces of gravity and drag.  The contextual discussion of Stokesâ law, drag coefficient and childhood experience provides a **specific and appropriate scientific and personal context**, fulfilling the highest descriptor.

Uncertainties in mass, time and velocity are included and propagated once, yet uncertainties for radius, drop height and derived variables are omitted.  This shows **consideration with significant omissions**, matching the 3â4 descriptor but closer to the lower end.

Methodological considerations are described (controlled variables table, uncertainty sources, notes on air currents and balloon shape) but the student rarely **explains** why each factor matters or how it influences data sufficiency.  The detail reaches âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, so a lower score in the 3â4 band is appropriate.

Specific weaknesses (reaction-time error, balloon asymmetry, turbulence) are **described**, but the discussion of their relative impact on results is minimal, aligning with the 3â4 descriptor at its lower end.

The apparatus list, step-by-step method, drop heights and timing procedure give enough information that another student could replicate the investigation, but there are **ambiguities** (conflicting drop distances of 1 m and 2.5 m, unclear balloon replacement, vague âidentifying terminal velocityâ rule).  These minor omissions place the work in the âfew ambiguitiesâ bracket, justifying upper-middle band.

Averages, terminal velocities, % differences and an appropriate graph are calculated correctly; minor rounding inconsistencies exist, but the **processing is largely accurate and appropriate**, warranting the upper level of the 3â4 band.

Improvements such as using video analysis and alternative methods are **described** but not fully explained in terms of how they would reduce the identified errors quantitatively.  This matches the âdescribedâ level within the 3â4 band.

Raw and processed data tables are present with units and uncertainties; the scatter graph is labelled and readable.  Some formatting issues exist but overall **communication is clear and mostly precise**, satisfying the mid-high band.

The student compares experimental results to Stokesâ law and presents percentage differences, yet the explanation of why drag coefficient assumptions fail is brief.  This provides **some relevant comparison** but not a fully justified one, fitting the mid band.

The conclusion directly addresses the research question, cites the upward trend in terminal velocity with mass, and is quantitatively linked to experimental data and theoretical expectations, demonstrating **consistency and justification**.  Minor lack of statistical detail prevents full marks but remains in the 5â6 band.

annotations.comment: When presenting Stokesâ Law, the student should explain how approximating a balloon as a sphere affects drag calculations and discuss the validity of the spherical assumption.
In defining weight, consider including any uncertainty in mass measurements and noting that g may vary slightly with location to reinforce rigorous data analysis.
The description of the drag force formula omits the assumed value of the drag coefficient. Cite a source or justify the selected Cd to improve reproducibility.
The formula for terminal velocity contains a typographical error (âv2mgâ). Ensure the expression $$v_t = \sqrt{\tfrac{2mg}{\rho C_D A}}$$ is correctly presented for clarity.
Restating the research question with the specific mass increments is clear, but consider summarizing the range (10â70 g) more concisely to improve readability.
The alternative hypothesis assumes a constant drag coefficient without justification. Discuss why Cd is likely unchanged across mass variations or acknowledge uncertainty.
The null hypothesis is clearly stated and mathematically expressed, providing a solid basis for statistical testing.
Table 1 lists only mass as the independent variable. Consider including drop height and inflation diameter for clarity on all planned variations.
Uncontrolled variables are well identified but lack assessment of their relative impact. Quantify how air currents or balloon shape might affect terminal velocity.

The research question is clearly stated and situated within the physics of parachutes, linking childhood curiosity to gravity and drag principles. This provides a strong contextual foundation for the investigation.
The introduction effectively connects personal experience to the scientific context, enhancing engagement and demonstrating the relevance of terminal velocity in real-world skydiving safety.
The dependent variable measurement method clearly includes uncertainty for time and distance and the calculation formula, demonstrating clear and precise recording steps.
Controlled variables table repeats the same control rationale under each heading, reducing clarity. Specify how each variable is independently managed and why.
The drop procedure cites a 1.00 m height but later uses 2.5 m for velocity calculations. This inconsistency creates ambiguity in experiment reproducibility.
The method for identifying terminal velocity relies on a âno swayingâ indicator. Provide a quantitative rule or video frame analysis to ensure precise detection of vt.
The experimental description notes diameter variation without addressing how shape changes introduce systematic error. Discuss corrective steps when switching balloons.
Safety table covers generic concerns well but omits specific precautions for indoor air currents and lighting. Include environment-specific risk controls.
The raw data table includes uncertainties for mass and radius, demonstrating appropriate propagation preparation.
Uncertainty propagation formula is correctly applied, but the student should elaborate on the derivation and assumptions behind $$\Delta v = \tfrac{d}{t^2}\Delta t$$.
The example calculation of uncertainty for trial 1 is accurate and demonstrates proper propagation, strengthening the data analysis.
The scatter plot figure lacks axis labels for units and error bars. Include these details to improve clarity and precision in data communication.
Introducing the linear regression test is appropriate, but the student should present calculated slope, intercept, and p-value to complete the analysis.
The qualitative observations section is generic. Link specific observed behaviors (e.g., oscillation amplitude) to data patterns to enrich analysis.
Table of quantitative variables still lists distance as âadjustableâ though experiments used fixed 1 m and 2.5 m. Clarify the drop distance selection and consistency.
Raw data table comprehensively shows trials, averages, and uncertainties. This clear presentation facilitates precise analysis.
Calculation of average time is correctly demonstrated, reinforcing the processing steps with clear mathematics.
Comparison table of experimental and theoretical velocities is useful, but the student should discuss causes of the consistent ~55% discrepancy in the conclusion.
Evaluation lists specific weaknesses but does not explain their relative impact on results. Rank or quantify which errors most influenced the data.
Hypotheses (Hâ and Hâ) are clearly stated, providing a strong statistical framework for t-testing the relationship between mass and velocity.
Table of methodological weaknesses and improvements is descriptive but lacks depth. Explain how suggested tools quantitatively reduce timing or drag errors.
The strengths table clearly identifies effective variable control and data precision, reinforcing confidence in the investigationâs reliability.
The conclusion succinctly ties experimental findings to Stokesâ Law and Newtonâs Second Law, demonstrating a justified and consistent interpretation.
Improvement suggestions are general; describe specific protocols (e.g., high-speed camera settings) to quantitatively reduce reaction-time uncertainties.
The proposed extension investigating material and shape effects on Cd is creative and would broaden the study, showing strong forward planning.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
